did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) deep learning model to perform multi-class skin cancer classification on the HAM10000 dataset. The study employed data balancing techniques (Random Oversampling), 5-fold cross-validation, and achieved 99% accuracy in classifying seven types of pigmented skin lesions.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and training of a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) on the HAM10000 dataset. It details specific methodology (data balancing, 5-fold cross-validation), reports new quantitative results (99% accuracy, per-class metrics), and includes a confusion matrix and performance comparison, which are characteristic of primary research rather than a literature review.

title: Deep Learning–Based Skin Cancer Classification Using Convolutional Neural Networks with K-Fold Validation and Data Balancing on the HAM10000 Dataset
journal: International Journal of Sciences and Innovation Engineering
brief_summary: This study developed a custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) for multi-class skin cancer classification using the HAM10000 dataset, which contains 10,015 dermoscopic images across seven lesion categories. To address class imbalance and ensure robust evaluation, the methodology incorporated Random Oversampling for data balancing, advanced preprocessing, and 5-fold cross-validation. The model architecture included multiple convolutional layers with batch normalization, dropout, and adaptive learning-rate reduction. Experimental results demonstrated a high classification accuracy of 99% (specifically 98.97% average accuracy) with strong per-class performance confirmed by confusion matrices and classification reports. The findings indicate that the proposed lightweight CNN architecture provides reliable generalization and stability, offering a potential clinical decision-support tool for automated skin cancer screening.

lesion_types_studied.details: Benign lesion class; 6,705 images in original HAM10000 dataset; 1,295 correct predictions in confusion matrix analysis.
Malignant lesion class; 1,113 images in original HAM10000 dataset; 1,323 perfect classifications in confusion matrix analysis.
Benign lesion class; 1,099 images in original HAM10000 dataset; 1,348 correctly classified samples with 3 misclassified into Actinic Keratosis.
Malignant lesion class; 514 images in original HAM10000 dataset; 1,381 correct classifications with 7 samples misclassified as Actinic Keratosis.
Precancerous lesion class; 327 images in original HAM10000 dataset; 1,242 correct predictions with minor misclassifications into Nevus, Melanoma, BKL, Dermatofibroma, and Vascular Lesions.
Benign lesion class; 115 images in original HAM10000 dataset; 1,392 correct predictions (flawless classification).
Benign lesion class; 142 images in original HAM10000 dataset; 1,292 correct predictions with zero errors.

all_task_types.target: Seven diagnostic categories of pigmented skin lesions (Melanocytic Nevus, Melanoma, Benign Keratosis, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Actinic Keratosis, Dermatofibroma, Vascular Lesions)
all_task_types.details: Multi-class classification of dermoscopic images into discrete diagnostic categories using a custom CNN on the HAM10000 dataset. The model predicts class labels with reported accuracy of 99% (98.97% average across folds) and per-class metrics (precision, recall, F1-score) via confusion matrices.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000 (Human Against Machine with 10,000 training images)
datasets_used.description: A public dataset sourced from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) archive, containing dermoscopic images of various pigmented skin lesions. It is a multi-institutional repository known for strong class imbalance.

use.training: Used for training the custom CNN model after preprocessing, data augmentation, and class balancing via Random Oversampling.
use.validation: Used for validation during the 5-fold cross-validation process.
use.testing: Used for testing and performance evaluation (confusion matrix, classification report) during the 5-fold cross-validation process.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To evaluate the generalization ability of the CNN model, ensure performance is not dependent on a single train-test division, and provide a reliable estimate of true accuracy by averaging results across five distinct data splits.
To address class imbalance in the HAM10000 dataset by duplicating minority class images, ensuring a more uniform distribution of samples during training to prevent bias toward majority classes.
To visualize and quantify classification performance, identifying true vs. predicted labels, class-wise strengths, and misclassification patterns across all seven lesion categories.
To summarize overall model performance (Precision, Recall, F1-Score) across all classes, accounting for class distribution in the averaged scores.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., t-tests, ANOVA, p-values) was reported in the paper. The study relied on descriptive statistics, including accuracy, precision, recall, F1-score, and confusion matrices, along with 5-fold cross-validation for performance evaluation.

confusion_matrix_details.Benign Keratosis: 1,348 correctly classified samples, 3 misclassified into Actinic Keratosis
confusion_matrix_details.Basal Cell Carcinoma: 1,381 correct classifications, 7 samples misclassified as Actinic Keratosis
confusion_matrix_details.Actinic Keratosis: 1,242 correct predictions, minor spillovers into Nevus, Melanoma, BKL, Dermatofibroma, and Vascular Lesions

HAM10000.notes: The model achieved an overall accuracy of ~99%. The study highlights that the proposed CNN, combined with Random Oversampling and 5-Fold Cross-Validation, demonstrated high stability and reduced variance compared to conventional architectures. The confusion matrix analysis revealed minimal misclassification between visually similar lesion types.

classification_or_output_head.type: Fully connected Dense layer with SoftMax activation

preprocessing_techniques: Normalization of pixel intensities to the range 0–1 by dividing by 256
Label reshaping to match model output format (one-dimensional arrays)
Image reshaping into four-dimensional arrays for input
Data augmentation: horizontal and vertical flipping

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Applied to help the model recognize lesions regardless of orientation.
Applied to simulate real-world variations in image capture angles.
Applied to introduce variations in lesion size and position while preserving key diagnostic features.
Applied to handle differences in lighting and color intensity.

conclusions: The proposed custom Convolutional Neural Network (CNN) model achieved a high average classification accuracy of 98.97% (approximately 99%) on the HAM10000 dataset, outperforming baseline models such as VGG16 (~88–90%), ResNet50 (~90–92%), MobileNetV2 (~85–88%), and Hybrid CNN + SVM (~80–85%).

The integration of Random Oversampling for data balancing and 5-fold cross-validation effectively addressed class imbalance and ensured robust generalization, resulting in high stability and reduced variance compared to conventional architectures.

The model demonstrated strong per-class performance with macro and weighted average precision, recall, and F1-scores all reaching 99%, indicating reliable classification across all seven pigmented skin lesion categories.

Specific lesion types such as Melanocytic Nevus, Melanoma, Basal Cell Carcinoma, Dermatofibroma, and Vascular Lesions were classified with near-perfect accuracy (100% or near 100% recall/precision), while Actinic Keratosis showed slightly lower recall (92%) due to minor misclassifications into other classes.

The lightweight custom CNN architecture provides a computationally efficient alternative to heavy transfer learning models, making it suitable for practical deployment in resource-constrained clinical settings.
The study concludes that the proposed AI-assisted system can support dermatological diagnostic workflows, potentially improving early diagnosis rates and reducing clinical workload.
important_remarks: The study relies exclusively on the HAM10000 dataset, which is known for significant class imbalance and limited diversity in imaging devices and patient demographics, raising concerns about external validity and generalizability to broader clinical populations.
The paper does not report key training hyperparameters such as the optimizer, loss function, number of epochs, or batch size, limiting the reproducibility of the custom CNN architecture.

Data leakage risk exists due to the use of Random Oversampling (duplication of minority class images) combined with 5-fold cross-validation; if oversampling was applied before splitting the data, information from the validation folds could have leaked into the training folds.

The input image resolution was reduced to 28x28x3 pixels, which is unusually low for dermoscopic analysis and may discard fine-grained diagnostic features critical for distinguishing visually similar lesions.
The paper lacks formal statistical hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) to validate the significance of performance improvements over baseline models.
No external validation was performed; the model was only evaluated on the HAM10000 dataset, which limits the assessment of its robustness across different clinical settings or imaging protocols.
The claimed 'lightweight' nature of the model is not substantiated with computational metrics such as FLOPs, parameter count, or inference time, making deployment feasibility claims speculative.
